narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 31, upper-middle family income, and your plan to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3 both sitting at the 2nd decile—meaning your early grades placed you in the lower end compared to your peers, where averages hover around the 5th or 6th decile. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted an 82.3% chance that you would fail it on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from past students. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile, as being in the lower ranks there strongly signaled higher risk compared to students who climbed higher. Your Year 1 decile played a significant supporting role in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early academic performance relative to the typical student's stronger standing. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, showing it as a relative strength in your profile, though not enough to offset the others. Compared to average applicants—who often have LSAT scores around 37, GPAs near 3.2, and mid-level deciles—your metrics aligned more closely with those who struggled on the bar, especially in those early grading deciles. Full-time status is common and didn't sway things much either way, as over 90% of students share that path. While family income in the upper-middle range is solid, it wasn't a top driver here. This rejection stings, especially given the barriers Hispanic students like yourself can face in competitive fields, but it's not the end of your journey. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting metrics like LSAT and GPA through targeted prep or additional coursework, perhaps retaking the LSAT or pursuing a post-bac program to demonstrate growth. Consider schools with holistic reviews that weigh your unique story more heavily, and gather strong letters highlighting your resilience. Keep pushing—you have the drive, and with refined preparation, you can shift the odds in your favor next time.
